title: Swap and Adjust Endpoints
subtitle: {key:o} jumps to the other end. {key:O} switches corners in block mode.
summary: While in *visual mode*, {key:o} swaps the cursor between the start and end of the selection — letting you adjust either endpoint without restarting. In *visual-block mode*, {key:O} swaps the diagonal corner.

blocks.text: Once in *visual mode*, the cursor sits at one end of the selection — and that's the *only* end you can extend. The {key:o} key swaps which end is active.

blocks.label: Adjust the start of a selection

sequence.note: Extend down past where you want
Now adjust the start with motions

blocks.title: Why Two Anchors in *Visual-block Mode*
blocks.text:
A character or line Visual selection has only two endpoints (start and end), so {key:o} has just one place to send the cursor. A *block* Visual selection has four corners — top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right.

  • {key:o} swaps the cursor to the *opposite corner* (diagonal swap).
  • {key:O} swaps to the corner on the *same row but other column* (horizontal swap).

Between them you can land on any of the four corners and extend in any direction.
Mnemonic: lowercase {key:o} = *opposite* corner; capital {key:O} = *Other* side of the same row.
